en.extract:
The Malaita cicadabird (Edolisoma tricolor) is a passerine bird in the family Campephagidae that is endemic to the island of Malaita in the Solomon Islands archipelago. It was formerly considered to be a subspecies of the Solomons cicadabird.


Taxonomy
The Malaita cicadabird was formally described in 1931 by the American ornithologist Ernst Mayr based on specimens collected on the island of Malaita in the Solomon Islands archipelago during the Whitney South Sea Expedition. Mayr considered his specimens to be from a subspecies of the Solomons cicadabird and coined the trinomial name Edolisoma holopolium tricolor. The bird is now elevated to species rank based on a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2018 and the differences in plumage and vocalization.

The '''Malaita cicadabird''' ('''''Edolisoma tricolor''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Campephagidae]] that is endemic to the island of [[Malaita]] in the [[Solomon Islands (archipelago)|Solomon Islands archipelago]]. It was formerly considered to be a [[subspecies]] of the [[Solomons cicadabird]].

==Taxonomy==
The Malaita cicadabird was [[species description|formally described]] in 1931 by the American ornithologist [[Ernst Mayr]] based on specimens collected on the island of [[Malaita]] in the [[Solomon Islands (archipelago)|Solomon Islands archipelago]] during the [[Whitney South Sea Expedition]]. Mayr considered his specimens to be from a [[subspecies]] of the [[Solomons cicadabird]] and coined the [[trinomial name]] ''Edolisoma holopolium tricolor''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Mayr | first=Ernst | author-link=Ernst Mayr | year=1931 | title=Birds collected during the Whitney South Sea Expedition. 17, The birds of Malaita Island (British Solomon Islands) | journal=American Museum Novitates | issue=504 | page=18 | hdl=2246/4238 | url=http://hdl.handle.net/2246/4238 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=192 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480867 }}</ref> The bird is now elevated to species rank based on a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2018 and the differences in plumage and vocalization.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Pedersen | first1=M.P. | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Joseph | first3=L. | last4=Rahbek | first4=C. | last5=Jønsson | first5=K.A. | date=2018 | title=Phylogeography of a 'great speciator' (Aves: ''Edolisoma tenuirostre'') reveals complex dispersal and diversification dynamics across the Indo-Pacific | journal=Journal of Biogeography | volume=45 | issue=4 | pages=826–837 | doi=10.1111/jbi.13182| bibcode=2018JBiog..45..826P | hdl=11250/2593769 | hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Bristlehead, butcherbirds, woodswallows, Mottled Berryhunter, ioras, cuckooshrikes | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=21 September 2024 }}</ref>
